coredata.dc:title: Boron regulated dual emission in B, N doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Budak, Esranur
Ünlü, Caner
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots are promising candidates for LED and solar cell studies with their low toxicity, inexpensive production cost and high luminescence capacity. However, controlling optical properties of graphene quantum dots is a challenge, which can be partially done by heteroatom doping. In this study, we synthesized dual emissive boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots through a bottom-up synthesis method. Dual emission properties of boron and nitrogen doped quantum dots appeared in the presence of high amount of boron precursor in synthesis medium. Dual emissive features of quantum dots were controlled via controlling amount of boron content in quantum dot structure. Boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots showed dual emissive properties at different excitation wavelengths, therefore they were suitable for applications concerning white – LED and solar cells. With their low toxicity, low production cost and controllable dual emission properties, dual emissive boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots can be considered as a powerful alternative to cadmium and indium based quantum dots.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and purification of boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Optical and structural characterization of B,N:GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Istanbul Technical University – Scientific Research Projects Unit (ITU-BAP) [ TGA-2019-41,938 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of APBA, BA, B,N:GQD-100BA and B,N:GQD-500BA

Absorption and emission spectra of a) B,N:GQD-APBA and b) B,N:GQD-500BA. QDs dissolved in ultrapure water. Absorption spectra was normalized to 1 at λabsorption = 320 nm. All emission spectra are collected with λexc = 350 nm and normalized to 1 at highest peak point.
Absorption spectrum of B,N:GQDs with different amount of boric acid.
Emission spectra of B,N:GQD-APBA at λexc = 350 nm and at λexc = 400 nm (left) and Emission spectra of B,N:GQD-APBA at λemms = 425 nm and λexc = 485 nm (right).
Emission spectrum of B,N:GQDs with different amount of boric acid a) λexc = 350 nm and b) at λexc = 400 nm.
Emission spectrum of B,N:GQDs with different amount of boric acid a) λemms = 425 nm and b) at λemms = 485 nm.

head.ce:title: Boron regulated dual emission in B, N doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istanbul Technical University, Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Maslak, 34469, Istanbul, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Istanbul Technical University, Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Maslak, 34469, Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Istanbul Technical University Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (ITUnano)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Istanbul Technical University Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (ITUnano), Istanbul, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Istanbul Technical University, Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Maslak, 34469, Istanbul, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots are promising candidates for LED and solar cell studies with their low toxicity, inexpensive production cost and high luminescence capacity. However, controlling optical properties of graphene quantum dots is a challenge, which can be partially done by heteroatom doping. In this study, we synthesized dual emissive boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots through a bottom-up synthesis method. Dual emission properties of boron and nitrogen doped quantum dots appeared in the presence of high amount of boron precursor in synthesis medium. Dual emissive features of quantum dots were controlled via controlling amount of boron content in quantum dot structure. Boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots showed dual emissive properties at different excitation wavelengths, therefore they were suitable for applications concerning white – LED and solar cells. With their low toxicity, low production cost and controllable dual emission properties, dual emissive boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots can be considered as a powerful alternative to cadmium and indium based quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots are carbon based semiconductor nanocrystals with size less than 10 nm, bright photoluminescence, low cost production rate and low toxicity [1–6]. They have been studied extensively [1–6] and used in many applications which involve development of LED, solar cells, chemosensor and in vivo protein tracking agents [7–16]. Graphene quantum dots possess unique structure with almost 2-D geometry and have been synthesized mostly through incomplete carbonization of carbon precursors (such as citric acid) with hydrothermal synthesis methods [1,6,10,17]. Due to characteristics of hydrothermal synthesis methods that have been used to synthesize graphene quantum dots (long duration, high synthesis temperature, etc …), to control optical properties of graphene quantum dots has become a challenge [10,12]. However, several methods have been developed to manufacture graphene quantum dots with controllable optical properties, such as doping graphene quantum dots with different atoms or using different carbon precursors and different synthesis media [18–24].

Studies on heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots revealed that many optical properties of graphene quantum dots, such as photoluminescence wavelength, quantum yield, etc …, can be controlled by doping different atoms to graphene quantum dot structure [19,22,25–27]. Especially, doping graphene quantum dots with sulphur (S), nitrogen (N) and boron (B) causes dramatic changes in photophysical properties of graphene quantum dots [19,22,25–27]. For instance, Li et al. showed that double doping graphene quantum dots with Mg and N causes enhancement of photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots [25]. In a different study, it is proposed that quantum yield of graphitic quantum dots enhances up to 71% due to doping quantum dots with oxygen and S [26]. Also, Budak et al. proved that doping graphene quantum dots with B and N improves quantum yield of graphene quantum dots and causes manageable shifts in emission wavelength of quantum dots [22]. In another recent study, dual emissive graphene quantum dots are synthesized by doping graphene quantum dots with phosphorus and nitrogen [27]. Liu et al. showed that red light emissive graphene quantum dots are obtained through doping graphene quantum dots with S, N and B [19].

Dual emission with quantum dots has been observed in different types of quantum dots including cadmium based quantum dots, indium based quantum dots and carbon based quantum dots [27–33]. Amongst all, dual emissive carbon based quantum dots have become center of attention due to their wide application range in sensing technology [27–33]. Recently, Kainth et al. synthesized dual emissive graphene quantum dots and investigated their potential as iron ion detector [28]. In a different study, Jalili et al. synthesized dual emissive graphene quantum dots and used them as a detection agent for penicillin [29]. In addition to sensing studies, Parvin et al. synthesized dual emissive P, N doped graphene quantum dots as an in vitro tissue imaging agent [27]. Also, dual emissive graphene quantum dots were synthesized for different purposes such as mercury (II) detection [30], arginine detection [31], fluorescent agents for in vivo cell imaging [32] and temperature detection [33]. However, there are only a few studies on controlling dual emission in quantum dots and to control dual emission in all types of quantum dots has been still a challenge.

In this study, we synthesized a novel dual emissive boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot by using a modified hydrothermal synthesis method with aminophenylboronic acid (as C, B and N precursor) and boric acid (as extra boron precursor). Without boric acid in synthesis medium, single emission peak was observed at 430 nm and 485 nm upon excitation at 350 nm and 400 nm, respectively. As boric acid was added to synthesis medium, dual emission property was prompted in B, N doped graphene quantum dots. Dual emission was observed at 430 nm and 485 nm upon excitation at both 350 nm and 400 nm. As boric acid amount was changed, the ratio of emission intensity at 430 nm and 485 nm changed. So, it was concluded that dual emission properties of B, N doped graphene quantum dots could be controlled by controlling amount of boric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis of boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (B,N:GQDs), 1 mg 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA, carbon, nitrogen and boron precursor) was dissolved in 10 ml distilled water, poured into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and sealed. Then, the autoclave was placed in furnace oven (Brand: Isolab) and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After 24 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature and yellowish aqueous product was obtained. Same procedure was repeated by adding 50 mg, 100 mg, 300 mg and 500 mg boric acid (BA, extra boron precursor) into synthesis medium, where 1 mg APBA was kept constant. As BA amount increased in synthesis medium, the final solution became colourless. The quantum dots (QDs) synthesized by different amount of BA was abbreviated as followed; B,N:GQDs synthesized by 1 mg APBA is B,N:GQD-APBA, B,N:GQDs synthesized by 1 mg APBA and 50 mg BA is B,N:GQD-50BA, B,N:GQDs synthesized by 1 mg APBA and 100 mg BA is B,N:GQD-100BA, B,N:GQDs synthesized by 1 mg APBA and 300 mg BA is B,N:GQD-300BA and B,N:GQDs synthesized by 1 mg APBA and 500 mg BA is B,N:GQD-500BA

In order to get rid of unreacted BA, initially aqueous quantum dots were kept at 1 °C for 24 h. The unreacted BA was precipitated during this procedure. Then the solutions were filtered through using qualitative filter paper with 10 μm pores and special filter paper with 0,22 μm pores. The filtration procedure was repeated twice. After that, quantum dots were centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 30 min in order to get rid of remaining big clusters and aggregates.
To obtain solid form of quantum dots, quantum dot solution was dried in a drying-oven at 40 °C. Solid quantum dots were kept in refrigerator for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of B,N:GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study optical properties of B,N:GQD, absorption spectra of QDs were recorded by Scinco Neosys-2000 double-beam ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. Emission and excitation spectra of quantum dots were recorded by Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorimeter. Emission spectrum of each sample was collected by using excitation wavelength (λexc) 350 nm and 400 nm. Each sample was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water after purification and diluted until optical density was under 0.1 in order to prevent errors resulting from self-absorption. All optical characterizations were performed on aqueous solutions of QDs. Excitation spectrum of each QD was recorded at emission wavelength (λemms) of 485 nm and 425 nm (the peaks in emission spectrum of QDs) and each spectrum was normalized to 1 at its most intense point.
For structural analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used.

In order to understand bonding characterization of B,N:GQDs, quantum dots were characterized by attenuated total reflectance FTIR spectroscopy. FTIR spectrum of each purified quantum dot was recorded in the range of 3750–750 cm−1 by using a PerkinElmer ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer.
Shape and size of quantum dots were determined by using TEM. TEM measurements were carried out with JEOL JEM 1220 at an accelerate voltage of 100 kV.
Synthesis and structural characterization of B, N doped graphene quantum dots:

B,N doped graphene quantum dots (B,N:GQDs) are synthesized through a modified hydrothermal synthesis method [34] which is widely used to synthesize boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots [34,35]. In principle, APBA serves as a single precursor which acts as carbon, nitrogen and boron precursor in this particular synthesis method [34]. In different studies, this synthesis method is modified to add an extra element doping to boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots by adding a different element precursor together with APBA (such as sulphur) [36,37]. In our study, the general synthesis method is modified to increase boron content in boron and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots by adding boric acid (extra boron precursor) together with APBA into the synthesis medium. All the other conditions were kept similar as was used in literature before [34–37].
Optical characterization of B,N doped graphene quantum dots:

Comparison of absorption and emission spectra of B,N:GQD-APBA to absorption and emission spectra of B,N:GQD-500BA revealed that as BA was introduced to the system, dual emission arose (Fig. 3 ). Both B,N:GQDs had a sharp peak at 230 nm and had a shoulder at 280 nm in their absorption spectra, which were due to π-π* transition of carbon, typical evidence of graphene structure (Fig. 3) [35]. However, the peak at 325 nm became more apparent in absorption spectrum of B,N:GQD-500BA (Fig. 3). It should be noted that the large stokes shift in B,N:GQDs is typical for graphene based quantum dots [34–37]. The peak at 325 nm in absorption spectrum appeared as a weak shoulder in absorption spectrum of B,N:GQD-APBA and was attributed to boron based differences in coordination environment [41]. As boron character in B,N:GQDs increased, the nanocrystal surface became more –OH group rich which was shown by FT-IR measurements and the peak at 325 nm in absorption spectrum became more apparent (Figs. 2 and 3) [41]. The emission spectrum of each B,N:GQDs showed characteristic features as well. Emission spectrum of B,N:GQD-APBA possessed a single emission peak at 425 nm upon λexc = 350 nm. On the other hand, emission spectrum of B,N:GQD-500BA possessed two distinct peaks at 425 nm and 485 nm upon λexc = 350 nm (Fig. 3).
Boron based dual emission in B,N doped graphene quantum dots:

When emission spectra of B,N:GQDs synthesized with different amounts of BA were compared, a clear change in ratio of intensity of peak at 425 nm to intensity of peak at 485 nm was observed due to increase in BA amount in synthesis medium (Fig. 6 ). For emission spectra of B,N:GQDs at λexc = 350 nm, the emission peak appeared at 485 nm slightly decreased as amount of BA in synthesis medium increased (Fig. 6). For emission spectra of B,N:GQDs at λexc = 400 nm, as BA introduced to synthesis medium, a new peak around 425 nm emerged and as BA amount increased, the intensity of the peak at 425 nm increased as well (Fig. 6). These results showed that dual emission in B,N:GQDs could appear at different excitation wavelengths and could be controlled by controlling BA amount in synthesis medium.

In order to understand the origin of dual emission in B,N:GQDs, PLE spectra of B,N:GQDs synthesized with different amounts of BA were compared at λemms = 425 nm and at λemms = 485 nm (Fig. 7 ). PLE spectra of B,N:GQDs synthesized with different amounts of BA showed characteristic features distinctly different than PLE spectrum of B,N:GQD-APBA (Fig. 7). For PLE spectra at λemms = 425 nm, as BA amount in syntheses medium increased, the intensity of minor peak at 260 nm increased and as BA amount became 500 mg in synthesis medium, the peak at 260 nm became one of the most intense peaks (Fig. 7). The peak at 260 nm could be attributed to increase in boron based structural features in B,N:GQDs. This result showed that, as the amount of BA was increased in synthesis medium, boron penetrates more in QD structure and after a certain amount, the peak at 230 nm, which appears due to π-π* in CC bonding [35], became less apparent and graphene structure became more like boron rich graphitic structure, which was also supported by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2). PLE spectra of B,N:GQDs synthesized with different amounts of BA at λemms = 485 nm showed more drastic differences when they were compared to PLE spectrum of B,N:GQD-APBA at λemms = 485 nm. The PLE peak at 290 nm was completely lost in B,N:GQDs synthesized with BA + APBA (Fig. 7). This major difference was followed by other drastic differences. In PLE spectra of B,N:GQDs synthesized with different amounts of BA, the peak at 395 nm became narrower and shifted to 345 nm, and became more intense as BA amount increased (Fig. 7). Finally, the peak at 230 nm shifted to 245 nm in PLE spectra of B,N:GQDs synthesized with different amounts of BA and when the amount of BA was 500 mg, this peak was completely altered to a new, narrower peak which was positioned at 260 nm (Fig. 7). These results showed that, as BA amount increased, the structure of QDs became completely different and boron based energy transitions started to became prominent, thus caused controlled dual emission by controlling BA amount.

In this study, we synthesized dual emissive B and N doped graphene quantum dots through a modified bottom – up synthesis method. High amount of boron precursor in synthesis medium caused formation of dual emissive quantum dots. Dual emission of B,N doped graphene quantum dots were manipulated by controlling boron precursor amount in synthesis medium. As the amount of boron precursor increased in synthesis medium, boron character in quantum dot structure increased and therefore caused dual emission. By controlling boron amount in quantum dot structure, dual emission properties of B, N doped graphene quantum dots were controlled.
This work was supported by Istanbul Technical University – Scientific Research Projects Unit (ITU-BAP) [TGA-2019-41,938].

Esranur Budak: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Caner Ünlü: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Dr. Ahmet Gül for support and letting use of UV-VIS spectrometer. We also thank Dr. Bünyamin Karagöz and his laboratory crew for support and letting use of Fluorescence Spectrometer. We would like to thank Dr. İsmail Koyuncu for the FTIR measurements and data analysis. The FTIR measurements and data analysis were performed in MEM-TEK (National Membrane Technologies Research and Development Center). The TEM measurements and data analysis were performed in Eskişehir Osmangazi University – Central Research Laboratory and Application Center.

B Chem. (2017). doi:10.1016/j.snb.2017.04.092.

(2015). doi:10.1039/c4tc02111h.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dually emissive P,N-co-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and photoacoustic tissue imaging in living mice
